title: Diva Obsexion
authors: Federico De Zigno Riccardo Morrocchi Stefano Piselli
description: Diva Obsexion is a illustrated by Federico De Zigno Riccardo Morrocchi Stefano Piselli. Banned for tending to support a theme of sadistic force against women.
descriptionParagraphs: Diva Obsexion is a illustrated by Federico De Zigno Riccardo Morrocchi Stefano Piselli. Banned for tending to support a theme of sadistic force against women.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a illustrated, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Banned for tending to support a theme of sadistic force against women. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

banningParagraphs: Diva Obsexion entered censorship debates as a illustrated associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1995 in New Zealand, where Office of Film and Literature Classification classified, prohibited, or restricted. Banned for tending to support a theme of sadistic force against women. Banned for tending to support a theme of sadistic force against women.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
